speaker: interviewer
text: Survivors of the Shoah Visual History Foundation. November 15, 1995. Survivor Sol Urbach. Interviewer, Dina Cohen. City, state Flemington, New Jersey, USA. The interview will be in English. My name is Dina Cohen. Today is November 15, 1995. I am interviewing Sol Urbach in Flemington, New Jersey, USA, The interview will be in English. Could you please tell me your name and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Sol Urbach. Last name is U-R-B-A-C-H. At times during the war, I would be known as Solomon, but in this country I have use Sol, S-O-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name at birth was Solomon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Solomon, right. I'm born on October 25, 1925. I was born in Kalwaria-Zebrzydowski. Spelling of that town is Kalwaria, K-A-L-W-A-R-I-A dash Zebrzydowski-- Z-E-B-R-Z-Y-D-O-W-S-K-I-- in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where in Poland is Kalwaria-Zebrzydowski?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kalwaria would be about 30 kilometers from Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you grow up in Kalwaria?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I I was raised-- I was born in Kalwaria and my, my putting together of events lead me to believe I was there up to the age of three. At the age of three, my family has decided to move from Kalwaria. And at that time, we would have been just three children, the two older ones and I was-- a brother, sister, and I would be the third one. When we were three children, my family has decided to move to Romania where there was a sister of my mother's who lived there and she reported of the good fortune and good things that are happening to them in Romania, so my parents upped from Poland and went to Romania. And we lived in Romania for the next five years, from 1928 to 1933.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where in Romania was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in Sadagura, which is the-- a-- the other side of the river from Chernivtsi, which today falls into Ukraine, but at that time it was Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your life in Sadagura?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember some things, very few things. But I remember that we used to live across from a-- what was a rabbi's castle or a rabbi's court like. He would have been known as the Sadagura rabbi. And I remember living there. I remember the one trolley car that was running in Chernivtsi itself. But I don't remember many more things from Romania. I also know that in Romania, there were three more children born to my family, which made the family of six, six children. Four brothers and two sisters, three of them being born in Romania between the years of 1928 and 1933. And in Romania in 1933, the Romanians simply decided that they will not permit Polish citizens, which we were at that time. We obviously didn't emigrate to Romania legally or with permission, so we were illegally there. And so at one time, the Romanian authorities came to our house in 1933, and they told us to just take with us what we can carry. And they simply took us to the border of Romania and Poland, someplace in the Stanislavov area or near the area of Lviv. And took us-- the Romanian soldiers took us to the boundary and pointed to us that-- to the direction of Poland, and this is where we would go. Naturally, as we crossed the border, the Polish authorities caught onto this family of eight people, six children, two of them practically infants. And the Polish authorities, a Polish policeman, took this whole family. Being a Jewish family had a lot to do with on how we would be treated. And they simply took the family away and threw us into jail. Now, here we were.  We were Polish citizens by right, but yet, the Polish authorities threw us in jail. And I remember distinctly sitting in a jail with a window way up high with, with bars. And I remember being in jail at the age of eight as we came out of Romania. To the best of my knowledge, what happened is that the Jewish community outside of the jail, the Jewish community living in the Stanislavov Poland area, discovered that there is a Jewish family with so many children in jail. And they went to work and bailed us out of that jail.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we probably didn't spend more than a night or two at most in jail, and the Jewish community was instrumental in getting us out of that jail. And where did you go when you got out of jail?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we got out of jail, we-- evidently my family traveled on a shoestring. And they wound up in Lviv, which I remember being there for a number of days. And eventually, when he-- when they gathered up enough money, they got enough money to get on a train and go from Lviv to Krakow. And in 1933, we arrived in Krakow. And from there, we settled in Borek Falecki, which is an outskirt of the city of Krakow. My father was a tailor, and that's how he made his living for the family. We lived in a, in a house with two rooms was all we had for the whole family of eight. And I went to school, because I just-- because the children rather, just arrived from Romania, did not speak Polish, we could not just go to any public school in the area, because with the anti-Semitism as it existed in Poland at that time, and with me-- with the children not speaking the language, Polish, it would have been very difficult to go to a-- any public school, which was located across the street from us. But we decide to go to a school which was a all-Jewish student body in the city of Krakow, located in the Kazimierz in the middle of the city of Krakow. This was the- school name was [NON-ENGLISH] on [NON-ENGLISH] Street in Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- [CLEARS THROAT] --was your family religious? You said--

speaker: interviewee
text: My family was religious. All depends on, on who would say that, because I thought my family was religious, but I-- on the other hand, I know that my grandfather and my uncle would have not eaten in our house because we were not religious enough for, for their standards. My father came from the city of Auschwitz, which was known in Polish as the city of Oswiecim, the later, the later infamous the city of Auschwitz. My mother came from Kalwaria-Zebrzydowski. And as best I understand, this was a matched marriage in those days. And they-- later on, after they got married, they lived in Kalwaria.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- [CLEARS THROAT] --when you were growing up in Krakow, did you have non-Jewish friends as well as Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did have some non-Jewish friends. I remember more the Jewish friends, because in school, my classmates were all Jewish. But at home, I would have some non-Jewish friends. But it was a rather difficult situation, because we're speaking about the time of 1933 when I arrived in Poland. And anti-Semitism, at that time, has reached a high pitch. With the coming to the power of Hitler in Germany, the anti-Semitism was sweeping Europe in a big way, particularly Poland. And so I remember the unpleasantries that you would have to live with as a Jewish child in a non-Jewish neighborhood where I lived. And it was not uncommon for the non-Jewish children to throw stones, rocks, at the, at the Jewish children. On many occasions as I walked to school from Borek Falecki to Krakow, somehow the non-Jewish children knew that we were Jewish and they would throw rocks at us, at the Jewish children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anti-Semitism, we lived with anti-Semitism. It was one of those situations that we couldn't get away from. And it was especially true with Hitler's coming to power in Germany. Were you aware of Hitler at that young an age?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were aware of Hitler. The newspapers carried the stories. The radio, you would hear the broadcasts about Hitler. You would hear the broadcasts telling on how supportive many of the Poles, many of the Polish organizations were of Hitler.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you would hear of Hitler. You would hear about bad things happening here and there, but not, not so much as we felt the anti-Semitism coming from the Poles. Your family was quite large. Could you describe your family and your home a little more.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Our family of six children, as we were in 1933, were two sisters and four brothers. I had one older brother than myself, three years older. My sister, the older sister, was a year and a half older than I was. And my younger sister would have been younger than I was by two years. And the two youngest brothers were, were four and five years younger than I was. So when the World War II broke out, my, my brothers and sisters were ranging in age from eight or seven to 14, 15. My brothers, older brother's name was Samuel. My older sister was a Rifka Regina. Younger one was [? Anne ?]. The two younger brothers, one of them was Simon and the other one was Haskel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said your father worked as a tailor?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a tailor, and this is how he made his living. He also occasionally was a cantor. And I also-- I don't-- although I don't remember the arrangements, whether he was getting paid for being a cantor or not, but I do remember him praying and conducting services during holidays in, in temples in our neighborhood. [CLEARS THROAT]

speaker: interviewer
text: What were holidays like in your home, religious-- Since we just had to make a battery change in the camera, I'll repeat my last question. What where religious holidays like in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family was observant in practices and, and in observing holidays and attending religious services. It was a-- kind of a holy atmosphere in the house when holidays came. I remember attending temple or shul together with my grandfather. And this was an extremely pious group of people getting together, and celebrating, and observing holidays. The Jews living in Poland almost acted like if they lived in Israel, like if they were happy, extremely happy just being able to celebrate holidays, observe holidays. There was always plenty of food. Even if there was a shortage during the week, there was plenty of food supply for holidays. So it was an extremely joyous occasion. It was a happy time during the holidays, Jewish holidays, and observance.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you attended a Jewish school in Krakow. How long did you attend that school?

speaker: interviewee
text: That school I attended for six years in Krakow, in the city of Krakow. And that meant that I walked from my house in Borek Falecki for about one hour. It took one hour of walk from my home to the school. And after attending school in the city of Krakow, I also attended cheder, a religious school in Podgorze, which is the other side of the river of Krakow, on [NON-ENGLISH]. And this would have been about the same spot where Oskar Schindler was later on riding a horse and witnessing what was taking place in the ghetto. The ghetto could be seen from that spot on [NON-ENGLISH] where I attended the Hebrew school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if you attended school for six years, that, that brings us to 1939, I guess.

speaker: interviewee
text: That brings us to 1939. That's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about September of 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: September 1, 1939, the World War II broke out. And after listening to all the propaganda on how short of a war this will be and how quickly the Poles will win the war, the, the French, the English will enter the war quickly on short notice and Germany will be defeated in no time at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we really did not concern ourselves too much. On the other hand, as I said before, the Jews sort of lived in Poland like in their own world. They were really extremely happy with being permitted to conduct their own lives, their own religious lives. And they didn't worry about politics of the country too much.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we were not overly anxious when, when war-- World War II broke out. It was one of those situations, it's going to start, it's going to end. And maybe nothing terrible will happen. But after a few days after September 1, 1939, we indeed saw the Germans coming into the city of Krakow. And I remember going out with other children to greet the German soldiers as they were arriving on their tanks, on their trucks. And they would set up some quarters on the-- along the road. This was in the summer, so they would set up some quarters. And when they began to dish out food, they in fact gave some food to the children that came to see them. So I was one of those kids that would receive food from the German soldiers as they were advancing into, into Poland. But of course, it didn't take very long before the war got uglier. And we, as a family, had to leave our house in Borek Falecki and run from there. And we didn't know exactly where to run, because it was-- mass confusion took over. And no one-- no one was there to tell us where we should go or where we shouldn't go. But the bombs were flying all around us, so we had to run some place. We-- we actually, as a family, we began marching towards the city of Krakow, which probably after rethinking, was the wrong place to go, because we should have gone more into the country. But we went to the city of Krakow hoping to escape the bombs. But that was not true. And in a short time after that, maybe two weeks into the war, we had to go back to our home in Borek Falecki and stay there from there on to continue the war. Life, life became very difficult immediately, two weeks after the war or so. It became very difficult, because number one, Jewish children were no longer permitted into any public schools. Food was in extremely short supply, so the family would rely on the children going and standing on line someplace where there-- where there were food-- where there was food available. We stood on line sometimes throughout the whole night to be able to buy a bread or milk or kerosene. And mostly the children had to do that, because my father with a beard could not stand in a line together with Poles, because he would be recognized as a Jewish person. And he would not be welcome in the line.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the children were best equipped to stand in those lines and not being detected as being Jewish. Sometimes we would stand as long as a-- as a-- most of the night on line to be able to buy bread in the morning. And sometimes that bakery would not have any bread anymore by the time we reached the end of the line. So life became unbearable. It was difficult to buy coal for fuel. That's how we got our heat. Kerosene for light, we needed kerosene in those days for our lights. Everything was in short supply.

speaker: interviewee
text: But as bad as it was, we did not realize immediately that there are worst things to come. We didn't have any idea that this would be worse on a daily, weekly basis. Now, with my whole family not being able to go to school, we were all at home. And the situation was more cramped than it was before, because we were all home with no place to go. My mother became the main food supplier. She had what they would say at time, an Aryan appearance. So she was able to get on a train much easier than my father would be able to do and travel to the country and buy some supplies and bring it home to feed the family. This became the major occupation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father able to continue working?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was able to work. But of course, it was-- there wasn't enough work anymore, so it-- money, money was also in short supply and things became much more difficult. In 1940, we still lived in, in Borek Falecki. Also by 1941, the order came out that all the Jews from the area, surrounding areas, would have to report to a ghetto, which was now created in-- outside of Krakow in Podgorze. My family did not immediately follow that order. Somehow we had the idea, or my parents had the idea, that maybe we will be able to stick it out and continue living in the Borek Falecki area for a while longer and maybe the war will be over. But that-- it just couldn't go much longer, because the neighbors would come in and tell us that they will not be able to keep, keep it a secret, that we should really get out of there and go to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after a short stay of trying to hide in the-- in Borek Falecki, my family picked up the belongings and they, at this time, had to smuggle themselves into the ghetto, because there was no legal way anymore of entering the ghetto. We had to actually find a way of illegally entering the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was at that time also that a fellow, a Polish cabinet maker for whom I worked. Said that he wouldn't mind if I remained after my family went to the ghetto. He wouldn't mind if I remained and worked for him. And he would try to supply me with food. And if I would stay in the workshop, in the furniture shop, I could continue living there. And my family evidently agreed to it. This was 1941, so I was almost-- I was 15 years old. My family evidently agreed to it that I would stay there. And I stayed on. This fellow's name was Kaminski, the furniture shop owner for whom I started working. And I stayed in the shop and slept. But now, because of the situation that I was left alone as a 15-year-old youngster, and I knew about the fear, I knew about the Germans. I knew that my family went to the ghetto. I was simply living in total fear. And during the night, when I slept on the workbench, the fear drove me absolutely insane. At times, I could've swore that somebody was knocking on my window. And all it was is a little kitten sitting on the outside of the window. But that drove me nuts, thinking that somebody's trying to break into the workshop. I simply-- I simply lived with fear. And when I could no longer stand it, I eventually went into the ghetto together with my family. So sometimes in 1941, I went into the ghetto with my-- and joined my family. We wound up in the ghetto, the family of us, of six children, parents wound up in the ghetto. Because we came into the ghetto very late, the housing facilities were no longer readily available, maybe as they were before.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were-- as a family of eight, we were assigned a room in a basement. And I remember that basement room. It was a horrible condition living there. But that's all we could get.

speaker: interviewee
text: Food was in very short supply. Hygiene facilities were nonexistent, or not to speak of anyway. You could not get a night's rest, because people were working at odd hours and coming back. And living eight people in one room just did not let us rest too much. But nevertheless, in, in truth, if I had to recall it, what we felt like, when we came into the ghetto, we somehow felt relieved that we are now amongst our own people and maybe the situation will be a little better than living outside of the ghetto and living in fear.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for a while, we, we thought, well, this is great. We're going to be amongst Jews and it's got to be better.
